questions.specification:
A triathlete monitors their weekly training volume and average resting heart rate (RHR) over a four-week periodized block, as shown in the table below.

| Week | Weekly Training Volume (hours) | Average Resting Heart Rate (bpm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 10 | 52 |
| 2 | 14 | 54 |
| 3 | 18 | 57 |
| 4 | 7 | 51 |

Which statement best explains the decrease in resting heart rate observed in Week 4 after the reduced training volume?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Week 4 shows a reduced training load (deload/taper) with resting heart rate dropping below the initial baseline, indicating recovery and a rebound consistent with supercompensation and recovery strategies.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The rising RHR across weeks 1–3 alongside increasing volume reflects accumulated fatigue; the subsequent drop in week 4 aligns with reduced fatigue after load reduction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is framed around a periodized training block and interpreting outcomes of load manipulation (increase then deload), which relates to training design principles.

options.content: The athlete is experiencing the negative effects of the principle of reversibility due to insufficient training stimulus.
options.markscheme: Week 4 shows a decreased RHR alongside a planned reduction in training volume (taper/deload), which is more consistent with recovery/supercompensation than detraining/reversibility.

options.content: The athlete is successfully recovering from accumulated fatigue to facilitate the process of supercompensation.
options.markscheme: Reduced training volume in Week 4 following progressive overload (Weeks 1–3) allows recovery from accumulated fatigue; the accompanying drop in RHR is consistent with a positive adaptation during taper/deload and supercompensation.

options.content: The athlete has reached a performance plateau because the training program lacked sufficient variety in exercise mode.
options.markscheme: A performance plateau is not directly evidenced by resting heart rate data, and the key observed change is a fall in RHR with reduced volume, suggesting recovery rather than lack of variety.

options.content: The athlete is demonstrating signs of chronic overtraining syndrome as the resting heart rate remains above baseline.
options.markscheme: In Week 4 the RHR (51 bpm) is below Week 1 (52 bpm), so it is not remaining above baseline; this does not support chronic overtraining syndrome.

questions.specification:
The training log for a cross-country runner shows the following adjustments over an eight-week period:

| Component | Month 1 | Month 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency | 3 days/week | 5 days/week |
| Intensity | $70\%$ Max HR | $85\%$ Max HR |
| Time | 20 minutes | 45 minutes |

Which training principle is best illustrated by the increased frequency, intensity and time from Month 1 to Month 2?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which training principle is demonstrated by increasing frequency, intensity, and time to drive further adaptation—this is the overload/progressive overload principle, covered under training design and principles.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: although the plan changes across time, the table most directly shows an increased training load (F, I and T).
Incorrect: reversibility refers to loss of fitness when training stops or is reduced, which is not shown.
Correct (C): frequency, intensity and time all increase, increasing training load to stimulate further adaptation.
Incorrect: variety refers to changing modes/types of training; the table shows increases in load variables rather than different activities.

questions.specification:
The table below shows the physiological results for an elite marathon runner before and after a five-week period of complete rest due to illness.

| Physiological Parameter | Pre-illness Value | Post-rest Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $$VO_2$$ max ($$ml \cdot kg^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}$$) | 78.2 | 69.5 |
| Stroke volume ($$ml$$) | 175 | 158 |
| Maximal cardiac output ($$L \cdot min^{-1}$$) | 32.5 | 28.2 |

Which quality of training is most clearly demonstrated by these data points?

options.markscheme: Neglecting recovery typically hinders physiological adaptations, as these processes occur during rest periods rather than during the training itself.

Recovery is essential for the replenishment of glycogen stores; neglecting it leads to chronic depletion.
Aerobic capacity ($VO_2 \text{ max}$) improvements require periods of recovery to allow the cardiovascular and respiratory systems to adapt to the workload.

- Improves the lipid profile by increasing HDL cholesterol and reducing LDL cholesterol and triglycerides <marks>A1</marks>

options.markscheme: Incorrect: planned recovery is important across ability levels to support adaptation and reduce injury risk.

options.content: They should be avoided because recovery time always reduces fitness gains from training.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: appropriate recovery supports adaptation; inadequate recovery can impair performance and increase injury risk.

questions.specification:
A student is preparing for a 6-week training block for futsal (indoor football). Before the block, the student completes a simple field-test battery and records the results shown in Table 1.

Table 1: Pre-training test results

| Test | Result | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Y-balance test (composite score) | 86% | Higher score indicates better balance |
| 505 agility test (turn on dominant leg) | 2.62 s | Lower time indicates better agility |
| Standing long jump | 1.92 m | Higher distance indicates greater lower-body power |
| Sit-and-reach | 29 cm | Higher score indicates better flexibility |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.2 Benefits to health of being active > A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (g) ask for physical and psychological benefits of regular exercise/being active, which aligns with active lifestyle benefits to well-being.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (f) links agility to injury prevention, matching strategies to minimize risk and improve the body’s ability to absorb forces.

parts.content: Identify two components of skill-related fitness relevant to futsal.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct component of skill-related fitness (any two).

Acceptable components include:
- Agility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Balance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coordination <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reaction time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Power <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Speed <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two physical benefits of regular exercise.
parts.markscheme:
- Improved cardiovascular health <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased muscular strength <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhanced flexibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved body composition (eg reduced fat mass/increased lean mass) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased bone mineral density/stronger bones <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct physical benefit (any two).

parts.content: Explain how balance, as indicated by the Y-balance test result in Table 1, can contribute to performance in futsal.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative marking points (award <marks>1 mark</marks> per valid explained point, up to 3):
- Better balance improves control/postural stability during dynamic movements (eg landing, pivoting, decelerating), supporting effective skill execution. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better balance helps maintain centre of mass over the base of support when being challenged by opponents, improving ball control/shielding. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better balance can reduce risk of falls and non-contact injuries by improving stability during changes of direction and single-leg actions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better balance supports rapid directional changes and transitions (eg attacking to defending) with fewer movement errors. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better balance can improve accuracy/efficiency of kicking/passing by stabilising the supporting leg. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for explaining relevant links between balance and futsal performance, including cause-and-effect.

parts.content: Explain how muscular strength can enhance athletic and daily performance.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative marking points (award <marks>1 mark</marks> per valid explained point, up to 4):
- Increased muscular strength increases force output, improving actions such as lifting, jumping, sprinting and tackling/holding off an opponent. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater strength improves joint stability and helps maintain posture/technique under fatigue or load. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strength supports daily tasks such as lifting, carrying, pushing/pulling and climbing stairs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased strength can reduce risk of musculoskeletal injury by improving load tolerance of muscles/tendons and supporting safer movement patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater strength can improve repeated-effort performance by reducing relative effort for submaximal tasks (ie a given task requires a smaller fraction of maximal force). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strength development can aid bone health via increased mechanical loading on the skeleton. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for explaining how strength improves performance in sport and daily life.

parts.content: Outline the role of agility, as indicated by the 505 agility test result in Table 1, in injury prevention and sport-specific tasks in futsal.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative marking points (award <marks>1 mark</marks> per valid outline, up to 4):
- Agility enables rapid changes of direction while maintaining body control, which is essential for evasion, marking, pressing and counter-attacks. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved agility helps avoid falls/collisions by allowing quick repositioning in crowded, dynamic play. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Agility improves responsiveness to external stimuli (eg opponent movement, ball deflections), supporting better decision-making in game situations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Agility enhances coordination during fast transitions between movements (accelerate–decelerate–cut). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better agility may reduce non-contact injury risk by improving deceleration and cutting mechanics (eg controlled knee/hip alignment) and reducing uncontrolled valgus/rotation moments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Agility supports effective defensive footwork to maintain balance and reduce risky reaching/overstriding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for outlining relevant roles of agility in futsal and/or injury prevention.

parts.content: Discuss how regular exercise can benefit psychological well-being for the student during the 6-week training block.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative marking points (award <marks>1 mark</marks> per valid discussed point, up to 4):
- Exercise can reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression, partly through endorphin release and improved affect. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exercise can enhance mood/emotional regulation via changes in neurotransmitter activity (eg serotonin, dopamine). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exercise can improve self-esteem and body image through skill mastery/achievement and perceived physical changes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exercise can reduce stress by lowering perceived stress and promoting relaxation/sleep regulation (eg reduced rumination, improved sleep quality). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exercise can enhance cognitive function (eg focus, attention) which may support academic performance and coping. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Participation in training can provide social interaction/support and a sense of routine/purpose, improving well-being. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for a balanced discussion of psychological well-being benefits (multiple perspectives/examples).

questions.specification:
A 17-year-old football (soccer) player is completing an 8-week pre-season programme.

Table 1: Selected fitness test results

| Test | Week 0 | Week 8 |
|---|---:|---:|
| Resting heart rate (beats min⁻¹) | 72 | 62 |
| Agility T-test (s) | 11.2 | 10.6 |
| Sit-and-reach (cm) | 24 | 29 |
| Push-ups in 60 s (reps) | 32 | 40 |

The player’s coach notes that match situations often require reacting quickly to an opponent’s pass and coordinating body movements while changing direction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.2 Benefits to health of being active > A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Flexibility training contributing to injury reduction links to strategies to minimize injury risk and improve force absorption.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Reaction time and coordination in dynamic team sports relate to nervous system coordination of physiological responses underlying quick reactions and coordinated movement.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explicit focus on reaction time connects to psychological refractory period as a motor-learning factor affecting reaction time performance.

parts.content: Using Table 1, state two long-term physiological benefits of cardiovascular training that are consistent with the player’s changes from Week 0 to Week 8.
parts.markscheme:
* Increased stroke volume (cardiac hypertrophy leading to greater blood ejected per beat) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Lower resting heart rate due to improved cardiac efficiency/greater parasympathetic tone <marks>1 mark</marks>

OR

* Increased maximal cardiac output (via increased stroke volume) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Improved capillarization (greater capillary density improving O₂ delivery) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Increased aerobic capacity/VO₂max (improved oxidative capacity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1 and the coach’s notes, identify two factors that affect agility performance in football.
parts.markscheme:
* Reaction time <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Balance <marks>1 mark</marks>

OR

* Coordination <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Muscle strength/power <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Speed (acceleration/deceleration ability) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, describe how flexibility training can contribute to injury reduction for this player.
parts.markscheme:
* Increases range of motion at joints (e.g., improved sit-and-reach suggests greater ROM) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Reduces muscle stiffness/increases compliance, allowing tissues to tolerate stretch better <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Decreases likelihood of strains/tears during football-specific actions (kicking, lunging, rapid changes of direction) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points:
* Improves movement mechanics/posture, reducing abnormal joint loading <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Can reduce muscle imbalance/tension that predisposes to injury <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: With reference to the coach’s notes, explain the role of reaction time and coordination in dynamic team sports.
parts.markscheme:
* Reaction time enables fast responses to opponents/ball flight, supporting earlier initiation of movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Coordination ensures accurate execution of complex movement sequences (e.g., foot-eye coordination for control and passing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Together allow fluid and efficient play when changing direction and performing skills under pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Important for high-speed decision-making situations where the correct response must be selected and executed quickly <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points:
* Better reaction time and coordination can reduce errors/turnovers and improve successful skill completion <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Helps maintain technical performance when fatigued or in unpredictable environments <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, outline the importance of muscular endurance in occupations requiring repetitive physical tasks.
parts.markscheme:
* Supports prolonged/repeated submaximal contractions with delayed fatigue (e.g., higher 60 s push-up score indicates improved endurance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Helps maintain technique and posture over time, reducing cumulative strain injuries <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Enhances productivity/work rate by sustaining output across a shift/task duration <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Important in roles such as manual labour, healthcare, military, or emergency services where tasks are repeated frequently <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points:
* Reduces need for rest breaks and supports safer handling/lifting over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Improves tolerance to repetitive loading, supporting resilience in the workplace <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how integrating skill-related and health-related fitness components could enhance this player’s overall match performance.
parts.markscheme:
* Health-related components (cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength/endurance, flexibility) provide a foundation for sustained effort and reduced injury risk across a match <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Skill-related components (agility, balance, coordination, reaction time) improve efficiency and precision of football-specific movements (turning, dribbling, passing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Integration allows maintenance of technical and tactical performance under fatigue (endurance supports repeated high-intensity actions; skill supports effective execution) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Enhances ability to respond to unpredictable stimuli (opponent/ball) and to select and execute appropriate actions quickly <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points:
* Balanced development improves movement economy/control, potentially improving repeated-sprint and change-of-direction performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Reduces risk of overuse injuries by distributing physical stress and improving overall conditioning and mechanics <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
● Increases cardiac output <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification:
A survey was conducted with 50 basketball coaches to examine the warm-up durations and components included in their sessions during the previous competitive season. Coaches also reported their focus on injury prevention through warm-up design. The following data were collected:

Figure 1: Warm-up duration used by coaches (% of total, with 95% confidence intervals)

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=lQjbCrisnQLUMsLjEBn4v)

Figure 2: Warm-up components used (% of coaches using each component, with 95% CI)

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=HeNTp54ZjMnl5oynAn7Y2)


The aim was to assess how warm-up practices reflect evidence-based principles of injury prevention and prehabilitation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question centers on warm-up design as an injury-prevention/prehabilitation intervention, comparing inclusion of components (strength, balance, agility, aerobic) and asking why some are less used—directly aligned with strategies to minimize injury risk and improve force absorption.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting how coaches structure warm-ups (duration distribution, component selection) reflects training-program design considerations for safe and effective preparation, beyond purely biomechanical injury mechanisms.

parts.content: Refer to Figure 1 and identify the most commonly used warm-up duration among surveyed coaches.
parts.markscheme: 10 min or less  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how warm-up durations are distributed among the coaches.
parts.markscheme:
- Most coaches selected 10 min or less OR 15 min or less as their warm-up duration  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Only a small proportion used durations greater than 20 minutes  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to Figure 2 and identify the warm-up component used by the fewest coaches.
parts.markscheme: Balance  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to Figure 2 and compare the inclusion rates of agility and strength components.
parts.markscheme:
- Agility is used by ~75–80% of coaches  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strength by ~65–70%  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the principle of prehabilitation applies to the consistent use of aerobic and strength components in warm-ups.
parts.markscheme:
- Prehabilitation involves targeting weaknesses or vulnerable joints to reduce injury risk  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Including aerobic OR strength components helps prepare the body by improving circulation OR muscle activation  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one reason why fewer coaches include balance exercises in warm-ups despite their known benefits.
parts.markscheme:
- Balance exercises are perceived as less important OR too time-consuming
- Some coaches may lack knowledge on their injury-preventive value  <marks>1 mark</marks>


<Note> Award  <marks>1 mark</marks> for any valid reason supported by logic.

questions.specification: Which programme design choice best supports safe training in youth athletes?

options.content: Gradual progression tailored to growth and development
Increasing training load by a fixed 10% each week regardless of growth and maturation
Maintaining high training volume year-round with minimal rest weeks to accelerate adaptation
Prioritising maximal-intensity sessions before mastering movement technique

questions.specification: What does reversibility imply for athletes returning from injury?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Reversibility is a core training principle/quality: when training is reduced or stopped (e.g., during injury), adaptations decline, affecting return-to-play training design.

options.content: Training adaptations are maintained even when training is reduced or stopped.
Fitness is lost when training is reduced or stopped.
options.markscheme: Correct: reversibility means training-induced gains decline when training stimulus is reduced or removed.

options.content: Fitness gains can be partially retained despite a reduced training load, but they will decline over time without sufficient stimulus.
Only aerobic endurance declines during detraining; strength is not affected.

questions.specification: Which training principle states that improvements occur only in the specific muscles and energy systems trained?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the training principle of specificity (improvements in the specific muscles and energy systems trained), which falls under training design/qualities of training.

questions.specification: Which training principle requires gradually increasing training demands to continue improving performance?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question tests understanding of a training principle (progression: gradually increasing training demands) within training design principles for safe and effective programs.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Specificity refers to training being specific to the sport, muscle groups, and energy systems required.
Correct. Progressive overload involves gradually increasing training demands (e.g., intensity, duration, frequency) to continue improving performance.
Incorrect. Progression is a general concept of advancing a training programme over time; the specific principle of increasing training demands is overload (progressive overload).
Incorrect. Recovery refers to rest and recuperation needed for adaptation and to reduce fatigue/injury risk.

questions.specification: Which of the following is a key characteristic of an effective training programme for elite swimmers?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about key characteristics of an effective training programme (progression, recovery, sport-specificity), which directly aligns with principles of training design for safe and effective programs.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)

options.content: Progression, adequate recovery, and sport-specific training
options.markscheme: Correct: effective programmes apply progressive overload, allow recovery/adaptation, and are specific to the sport.

options.content: High training volume every session with minimal variation in intensity
options.markscheme: Incorrect: consistently high volume with little intensity variation increases overtraining risk and reduces effective periodization.

options.content: Technique-focused sessions only, without planned progressive overload
options.markscheme: Incorrect: technique is important, but without progressive overload performance gains are limited.

options.content: Cardiovascular conditioning only, with strength training excluded throughout the season
options.markscheme: Incorrect: strength and power development is commonly integrated (appropriately periodized) to support swimming performance.

questions.specification:
A coach observes delayed recovery and an elevated core temperature in a female athlete.

Using the menstrual cycle image, where Stage 3 represents the post-ovulation (luteal) phase when progesterone is high and basal/core temperature is typically elevated, which stage is she most likely in and what adjustment should be made?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/AD6rSwlz_szfa8s-Ytl0k)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question links menstrual-cycle stage to hormone levels (notably progesterone/estrogen) and resulting physiological effects (elevated core temperature), which is endocrine regulation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It uses symptoms of delayed recovery and asks for a training adjustment (reduce load/increase rest), aligning with mechanisms of fatigue and recovery considerations.

options.content: Stage 1; reduce load or increase rest due to menstruation-related symptoms (e.g. cramping/low energy).
options.markscheme: Stage 1 corresponds to menstruation; temperature elevation is more typically associated with the luteal phase rather than menstruation.

options.content: Stage 2; reduce load or increase rest due to rising estrogen in the follicular phase.
options.markscheme: Stage 2 is the follicular phase; progesterone and basal/core temperature are not typically highest here.

options.content: Stage 3; reduce load or increase rest due to high progesterone and elevated basal/core temperature.
options.markscheme: Stage 3 is the luteal (post-ovulation) phase; progesterone is elevated and basal/core temperature is typically higher, which can increase thermal strain and may warrant more recovery/reduced load.

options.content: Stage 4; maintain or increase load because hormone levels are low and stable at the end of the cycle.
options.markscheme: Stage 4 is late luteal/premenstrual; hormones are changing (often falling), and the stem’s temperature cue aligns better with the mid-luteal period (Stage 3) than with a “stable” phase.

questions.specification: Why is progression important in training design?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.1 Qualities of training > A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1.1 Importance of training design for safe and effective programs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the principle of progression in training design—gradually increasing training demands for continued adaptation—which falls under the importance of training design for safe and effective programs.

options.content: To maintain the same training load indefinitely
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Progression involves planned increases/adjustments in training load rather than keeping it constant.

options.content: To gradually increase demands for continued improvement
options.markscheme: Correct. Progression means systematically increasing training demands over time to stimulate ongoing adaptation and improvement.

options.content: To adjust training variables over time to prevent a plateau
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This relates to progression/overload effects but is not the best definition here compared with systematically increasing demands for continued improvement.
Incorrect. Progression can involve manipulating many training variables (frequency, intensity, time, type, recovery), not only reducing rest intervals.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions

parts.content: State two components of health-related fitness.
parts.markscheme:
-  Cardiovascular endurance <marks>1 mark</marks>
-  Muscular strength <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note> Other acceptable answers: Muscular endurance, Flexibility, Body composition - any two</note>

parts.content: Identify two health risks of a sedentary lifestyle.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased risk of cardiovascular disease <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher likelihood of obesity <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>(Other acceptable answers: Type 2 diabetes, Poor mental health - any two)</note>

parts.content: Describe how cardiovascular fitness contributes to health.
parts.markscheme:
-  Enhances oxygen transport <marks>1 mark</marks>
-   Reduces resting heart rate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports endurance in daily tasks <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the importance of muscular strength in daily activities.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps with lifting, carrying, and posture <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces injury risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports skeletal alignment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves movement efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the benefits of improving flexibility for athletic performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Increases range of motion, allowing for more efficient and powerful movements <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances technique and form in sport-specific skills <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces the risk of muscle strains and joint injuries <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves recovery by decreasing muscle stiffness and promoting circulation <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note> Other acceptable answers: Contributes to better posture and movement economy; supports balanced muscular development </note>

parts.content: Discuss the impact of regular physical activity on reducing the risk of chronic diseases.
parts.markscheme:
- Improves insulin sensitivity, reducing the risk of type 2 diabetes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances cardiovascular health by lowering blood pressure and improving lipid profiles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps maintain healthy body weight, reducing the risk of obesity-related conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strengthens the immune system, lowering susceptibility to certain illnesses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces systemic inflammation, a contributing factor in many chronic diseases <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Other acceptable answers: Supports mental health, reducing risk of stress-related disorders; enhances bone density, lowering osteoporosis risk</note>
